one_line_takeaway: AgentRaft formalizes and automatically detects 'Data Over-Exposure' -- LLM agents leaking more data than users intended during ordinary, non-adversarial tool-chaining -- via a call-graph-guided prompt synthesizer plus dynamic taint tracking and multi-LLM voting, finding DOE present in 57% of tested source-to-sink call chains and 65% of over-exposed data fields across a 6,675-tool MCP testbed.
key_finding: Across all four scenarios, DOE is systemic: 347 of 608 extracted source-to-sink call chains (57.07%) exhibit DOE risk, 1,158 of 3,035 synthesized prompts (38.15%) trigger at least one DOE instance, and 1,803 of 2,756 transmitted data fields in DOE-triggering prompts (65.42%, ranging 63.28% in Social to 67.78% in Data) are over-exposed. Component-level results: the FCG generator achieves aggregate Precision 96.47% / Recall 93.77% / F1 95.10% across 107 nodes and 332 edges (Table 3); synthesized prompts achieve 93.74% aggregate trigger coverage (2,845/3,035) into the intended call chain (Table 4); the multi-LLM DOE judge reaches aggregate Precision 97.48% / Recall 98.25% / F1 97.86% on 100 sampled DOE-involving prompts per scenario (Table 5). In the Enterprise-only ablation (94 ground-truth DOE chains), multi-LLM voting substantially outperforms single-model judges: F1 97.92% for the committee vs. 83.71-84.15% for single GPT-4.1/DeepSeek-V3.2/Qwen3-Plus judges (Table 6), with false positives dropping from up to 42 to 4. Also in the Enterprise-only comparison, AgentRaft (with call-graph guidance and voting) reaches 69.15% ground-truth DOE coverage within 50 prompts and ~99% (93/94, exactly 98.94%) coverage within 150 prompts, versus non-guided random-search baselines that plateau near 18-20% even after 300 prompts, and a CG-guided-but-single-judge baseline (Baseline 3) that plateaus around 76.6%/58.51% coverage. Cost-wise (Enterprise, Table 8), AgentRaft's per-verified-chain token cost (0.012M tokens/chain) is an 88.6% reduction versus Baseline 2's 0.105M tokens/chain, and its projected full-coverage cost (1.2M tokens) is far below Baseline 3's estimated 8.2M tokens; the non-guided baselines were deemed infeasible to reach convergence at all.
core_contribution: AgentRaft, the first automated framework for detecting 'Data Over-Exposure' (DOE) in LLM agents -- a formally defined risk where an agent transmits sensitive data to a downstream tool beyond what is user-intended and functionally necessary, formalized as D_OE = (D_trans \ (D_nec ∪ D_int)) ∩ D_total. The framework has three modules that are the paper's own contribution: (1) a Cross-Tool Function Call Graph (FCG) built via hybrid static type-pruning + LLM-based dependency validation to model source-to-sink data-flow paths across heterogeneous tools; (2) call-chain-driven synthesis of high-fidelity user prompts (via a Verb+Object 'call edge' template) that deterministically drive an agent through a targeted multi-hop tool chain; and (3) a runtime module that applies dynamic taint tracking (labeling source-retrieved fields outside D_int as 'target') combined with a multi-LLM voting committee (GPT-4.1, Qwen3-Plus, DeepSeek-V3.2), grounded in GDPR/CCPA/PIPL data-minimization language, to judge whether transmitted-but-tainted fields are functionally necessary (D_nec) or constitute a DOE violation.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model. The paper explicitly distinguishes itself from prior LLM-agent security work that studies an attacker intentionally triggering a leak (e.g., prompt injection, malicious plugins); AgentRaft instead targets architectural/unintentional risk arising during normal, legitimate agent operation. The root causes it names are (1) overly broad data provision by tool design (tools return more fields than a task strictly needs) and (2) LLMs' lack of contextual privacy awareness (models fail to enforce a strict data boundary and pass whole records -- e.g., a full financial record including credit card number and CVV -- to a downstream sink like send_email when the user only asked for a payment date).
limitations: Paper's own stated limitations: (1) overall detection effectiveness is intrinsically bounded by the underlying LLM's reasoning quality -- hallucinations or reasoning failures in the FCG-construction, prompt-synthesis, or judging LLMs can degrade precision/recall; (2) FCG construction is static/semi-dynamic, so environments where tools are added or modified at runtime require frequent re-validation to keep the graph accurate. The paper also explicitly concedes it could not evaluate proprietary commercial agents because their internal execution environments are inaccessible for fine-grained runtime trace collection, so all four evaluated 'agents' were authors' own constructions built around real crawled MCP tools rather than existing production systems. Additional limitations inferred from the evaluation design (not the paper's own limitations section, flagged here as such): the head-to-head baseline comparison (RQ3/RQ4, the 69.15%/~99%/88.6% figures) is confined to a single scenario (Enterprise, 94 ground-truth chains) for cost reasons rather than run across all four scenarios; ground truth for the DOE judge (Table 5/6) and ablation baselines relies on manual labeling/cross-verification of sampled prompts and author-defined source/sink lists, introducing a degree of subjective/manual ground-truth construction; and D_int/D_nec/user-asset PII are synthetically generated by an LLM (DeepSeek-V3.2) rather than drawn from real user data.

datasets_used: 6,675 real-world MCP tools crawled from MCP.so
Four author-constructed agents built on the AgentDojo runtime, one per scenario: Enterprise Collaboration, Software Dev & IT Ops, Social Platform Communication, Data Management & Analysis
LLM-generated (DeepSeek-V3.2) synthetic user assets mixing simulated PII and business data
quantitative_results: DOE prevalence overall: 347/608 call chains (57.07%) involve DOE
DOE prevalence overall: 1,158/3,035 generated user prompts (38.15%) involve DOE
DOE prevalence overall: 1,803/2,756 transmitted data fields (65.42%) are over-exposed
FCG generation accuracy (Table 3, 107 nodes/332 edges): Precision 96.47%, Recall 93.77%, F1 95.10%
Prompt trigger coverage (Table 4): 2,845/3,035 prompts (93.74%) successfully trigger the targeted call chain
DOE judge accuracy (Table 5, aggregate across scenarios): Precision 97.48%, Recall 98.25%, F1 97.86%

Multi-LLM voting vs single-LLM judges (Table 6, Enterprise scenario, 100 sampled DOE prompts): voting committee F1 97.92% vs single-model F1 82.02-89.94% precision/recall band (F1 83.71-84.15%); FPs drop from up to 42 to 4

Enterprise-only baseline comparison (RQ3, 94 ground-truth chains): AgentRaft reaches 69.15% coverage at 50 prompts and ~99% (98.94%, 93/94) at 150 prompts; random-search baselines (Baseline 1/2) plateau at ~18-20% after 300 prompts; CG-guided single-judge Baseline 3 plateaus at ~76.6% (58.51% at 150 prompts per Table 8)

Enterprise-only cost overhead (Table 8): AgentRaft 0.012M tokens/verified chain vs Baseline 2's 0.105M tokens/chain, an 88.6% per-chain cost reduction; projected full-coverage cost 1.2M tokens (AgentRaft) vs 8.2M tokens (Baseline 3); Baselines 1/2 deemed infeasible to reach full coverage
baselines_compared: Baseline 1: random LLM-generated prompts + single-LLM (GPT-4.1) judge
Baseline 2: random LLM-generated prompts + multi-LLM voting judge
Baseline 3: call-graph-guided prompt synthesis + single-LLM (GPT-4.1) judge
Single-LLM judges compared individually: GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-Plus
